title: Danish PM Resigns After Coalition Suffers Major Election Defeat
description: Denmark's Mette Frederiksen steps down as coalition government falls, setting stage for complex power-sharing negotiations.
lede: Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen handed in her government's resignation following a crushing defeat in Denmark's snap elections on March 25. With neither left nor right-wing blocs securing a majority, the country faces weeks of complex coalition negotiations that could reshape Danish politics.

sections.heading: Coalition Government Falls
paragraphs: Mette Frederiksen's three-party coalition government suffered a decisive defeat in Tuesday's snap elections, forcing the Social Democratic leader to tender her resignation to Queen Margrethe II. The defeat marks a significant shift in Danish politics after Frederiksen's tenure as Prime Minister since 2019.

The election results showed Danish voters' dissatisfaction with the current government's handling of key issues including immigration, economic policy, and European Union relations. Frederiksen's Social Democrats, while remaining the largest single party, lost crucial support that had kept her coalition in power.

sections.heading: No Clear Majority Emerges
paragraphs: The fragmented election results present a complex puzzle for forming Denmark's next government. Neither the traditional left-wing bloc nor the right-wing opposition secured the 90 seats needed for a parliamentary majority in the 179-seat Folketing.

This outcome reflects the increasingly polarized Danish political landscape, where traditional party loyalties have weakened and voters have shifted toward more centrist or populist alternatives. The result mirrors broader European trends of political fragmentation and coalition complexity.

sections.heading: Moderate Party Holds Key
paragraphs: Lars Løkke Rasmussen's centrist Moderate party has emerged as the kingmaker in Denmark's political crisis. Ironically, Rasmussen currently serves as Foreign Minister in Frederiksen's own government, creating an unusual dynamic in the coalition negotiations.

The Moderate party's 16 seats could tip the balance toward either a left-leaning or right-leaning government. Rasmussen, a former Prime Minister himself, is known for his pragmatic approach and has previously worked with both sides of the political spectrum.

sections.heading: European Implications
paragraphs: Denmark's political uncertainty comes at a crucial time for European Union relations and Nordic cooperation. The country has played an important role in EU decision-making, particularly on climate policy, defense cooperation, and economic coordination with other Nordic nations.

Any new government will need to navigate Denmark's relationship with Brussels while addressing domestic concerns about sovereignty and immigration that influenced the election results. The outcome could affect Denmark's stance on further European integration.

sections.heading: Economic Concerns Drive Voter Sentiment
paragraphs: Rising inflation, energy costs, and housing prices dominated the election campaign, with voters expressing frustration over the government's economic management. Denmark's strong social welfare system faces pressure from demographic changes and global economic uncertainty.

The new government, regardless of its composition, will inherit significant fiscal challenges including pension reform, healthcare funding, and maintaining Denmark's competitive position in the global economy while preserving its generous social model.

sections.heading: Coalition Talks Begin
paragraphs: Frederiksen has indicated her willingness to remain as Prime Minister if she can assemble a viable coalition, but faces the challenge of reaching across traditional party lines. The negotiations could take weeks and may result in unusual political partnerships.

Queen Margrethe II will begin consultations with party leaders to identify who can command parliamentary confidence. The process reflects Denmark's mature democratic traditions while highlighting the evolving nature of European coalition politics in an era of increased voter volatility.

faqs.q: Why did Mette Frederiksen resign as Prime Minister?
faqs.a: Frederiksen resigned after her three-party coalition government suffered a major defeat in the March 25 snap elections, losing the parliamentary support needed to govern effectively.
Lars Løkke Rasmussen's centrist Moderate party holds the balance of power, as neither left nor right-wing blocs won a majority. His 16 seats could determine the government's composition.
Yes, if she can negotiate a new coalition agreement that commands parliamentary confidence. However, this would require reaching agreements across traditional party lines.

faqs.q: How long will coalition negotiations take?
faqs.a: Danish coalition talks typically take several weeks. The complex political arithmetic and need for cross-party cooperation could extend negotiations, similar to other European coalition governments.

faqs.q: What issues dominated the election campaign?
faqs.a: Economic concerns including inflation, energy costs, and housing prices were key issues, along with immigration policy, EU relations, and maintaining Denmark's social welfare system.
